children.name: Bos
children.description: The wild and domestic cattle of Eurasia and Southeast Asia, including the ancestors of all taurine and zebu cattle breeds as well as yak, gaur, banteng, and kouprey, central to human agriculture for millennia.

children.name: Bubalus
children.description: The Asian water buffaloes and their island relatives, including the domesticated river buffalo widely used as a draught animal in South and Southeast Asia, anoa, and tamaraw.

children.name: Bison
children.description: The bison, two species — the American plains and wood bison and the European wisent — which are the heaviest land animals in their respective continents and historically shaped entire grassland ecosystems.

children.name: Pseudoryx
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the saola, one of the most remarkable and elusive large mammals ever discovered — first described to science only in 1992 from skulls found in hunters' houses in the Annamite Mountains of Vietnam and Laos. Despite its bovid anatomy it is only distantly related to oryx; its closest living relatives are within the Bovini. The saola ('spinning wheel posts', referring to its long parallel horns) was the first large mammal new to science since the kouprey in 1937, and no Western scientist has ever observed one alive in the wild.

children.name: Pseudoryx nghetinhensis
children.description: One of the world's rarest and most enigmatic large mammals, known from fewer than a handful of camera-trap photographs and several captive individuals, all of which died within weeks of capture. Discovered in 1992 in the Vũ Quang Nature Reserve in Nghệ Tĩnh Province, Vietnam — the species name honours the province. It inhabits dense, wet forest in the Annamite Mountains on the Vietnam–Laos border. Critically Endangered; forest loss and snare hunting (intended for other animals) are the main threats. The global population is estimated at only a few dozen to a few hundred individuals. It has become a symbol of what may yet remain undiscovered in the world's remaining wilderness, and of the catastrophic biodiversity crisis facing Southeast Asian forests.

children.name: Tragelaphus
children.description: The spiral-horned antelopes of Africa — a genus of strikingly beautiful bovids in which males typically bear long, spiralled horns while females are smaller and hornless. Most are browsers or mixed feeders of forest and bush habitats. They include some of Africa's most charismatic large mammals: the kudu with its magnificent corkscrew horns, the sitatunga with its splayed hooves for swampy ground, and the bushbuck found across sub-Saharan Africa.

children.name: Tragelaphus strepsiceros
children.description: The greater kudu's male carries what many consider the most magnificent horns of any antelope — long, open spirals that can exceed 1.8 metres along the curve, taking 6 years to reach full development. Despite their impressive stature (bulls can reach 250 kg), kudus are elusive browsers of dense bush and woodland across eastern and southern Africa, relying on camouflage and freezing to avoid detection. Females and young form small herds; mature males are largely solitary except during the rut. Their bark alarm call is one of the most startling sounds of the African bush. The species name strepsiceros (Greek for 'twisted horn') is perfectly apt.

children.name: Tragelaphus spekii
children.description: An unusual antelope specialised for swampy, waterlogged habitats — its elongated, splayed hooves act like snowshoes on soft mud, and it readily wades and swims. Found in papyrus swamps, reed beds, and flooded forests across central and eastern Africa. Males are dark, shaggy and spiral-horned; females are smaller, bright chestnut and hornless. When alarmed, sitatungas retreat into deep water, submerging all but their nostrils. Named after John Hanning Speke, the British explorer who first reached Lake Victoria. Least Concern, but dependent on intact wetland habitats that face ongoing drainage and clearance.

children.name: Taurotragus
children.description: The elands — the world's largest antelopes, ox-like in build with distinctive dewlaps, tightly spiralled horns in both sexes, and a remarkable ox-like clicking sound produced by the tendons of their legs during movement. Despite their massive size (bulls can exceed 900 kg), elands are capable of jumping fences over 1.5 metres from a standstill. They have been domesticated experimentally in Russia and Zimbabwe for meat and milk production.

children.name: Taurotragus oryx
children.description: The largest antelope in the world, rivalling domestic cattle in mass — old bulls can reach 940 kg. Despite this size the common eland is a supreme jumper and long-distance traveller, nomadically following rainfall across southern and eastern African savannas and woodlands. Both sexes carry tightly spiralled horns. A mixed feeder of grasses and browse, elands produce extraordinarily rich milk and have been the subject of domestication experiments. The clicking of their leg tendons is used to maintain herd cohesion in dense vegetation. Least Concern; widespread and adaptable.

children.name: Connochaetes
children.description: The wildebeest (gnu) — two species of large, ungainly-looking antelopes of African savannas with distinctive bovine heads, maned necks, and slender legs. The blue wildebeest famously undertakes one of Earth's greatest wildlife spectacles: the annual Serengeti migration, in which over 1.5 million animals move in a circular 3,000-km circuit following rainfall. Both species have elaborate rutting displays involving frenetic running, rolling, and bellowing.

children.name: Connochaetes taurinus
children.description: The most abundant large antelope in Africa, with a total population exceeding 1.5 million. The Serengeti–Masai Mara wildebeest migration — in which over a million animals cross the Mara River in massive, crocodile-gauntleted columns — is one of the world's most dramatic wildlife events. Blue wildebeest are a keystone grazer of East and southern African savannas, their grazing maintaining short turf that benefits other species. Calving is highly synchronised — up to 80% of females give birth within a 3-week window — flooding predators and dramatically improving individual calf survival.

children.name: Connochaetes gnou
children.description: The black wildebeest (white-tailed gnu) is endemic to the Highveld grasslands of South Africa and Lesotho, distinguishable from the blue wildebeest by its darker coat, forward-curving horns, and white tail. It came extremely close to extinction in the late 19th century — reduced to under 600 animals by hunting and habitat clearance — and survived only through protection on private farms and game reserves. All wild black wildebeest are descendants of these rescued populations, and the species now numbers around 18,000. Conservation of South Africa's temperate grassland ecosystems is essential for its continued survival.

children.name: Oryx
children.description: The oryx — four species of large, powerfully built antelopes of African and Arabian desert and semi-desert environments, all bearing spectacularly long, straight or gently curved horns that gave rise to the unicorn legend (seen in profile, an oryx can appear to have a single horn). Highly adapted for arid conditions, oryx can tolerate body temperatures of 45°C and survive long periods without free water.

children.name: Oryx gazella
children.description: The largest of the four oryx species and an icon of the Kalahari and Namib deserts. Both sexes carry long, rapier-straight horns that can exceed 85 cm and have been used lethally against lions. The gemsbok's bold black-and-white facial mask and contrasting body markings are thought to reflect heat and reduce eye glare in its extreme desert environment. It can survive without drinking water for months, obtaining moisture from melons, roots, and succulent plants. Populations in the Kalahari undertake large movements following rainfall and can concentrate in thousands at water sources after rains. Introduced to New Mexico's White Sands area where feral populations have established.

children.name: Capra
children.description: The wild goats and ibexes — nine species of agile, rock-climbing bovids distributed from the mountains of western Europe through the Middle East to Central Asia. All share a talent for near-vertical rock faces, assisted by hard outer hoof edges for gripping and soft inner pads for friction. The domestic goat (C. hircus) is descended from the wild goat and has been kept by humans for over 10,000 years; it is now one of the most widely distributed domestic animals on Earth.

children.name: Capra aegagrus
children.description: The wild ancestor of the domestic goat, native to rocky mountainous terrain from the eastern Mediterranean through Turkey, the Caucasus, and Iran to Central Asia. Males carry the species' characteristic scimitar-shaped, keel-ridged horns that can reach 130 cm. The wild goat was domesticated around 10,000–11,000 years ago in the Fertile Crescent — one of the earliest domestic animals alongside sheep and cattle. Wild populations are now Vulnerable, reduced by hunting, disease transmission from domestic goats, and hybridisation. The Cretan subspecies (the kri-kri) survives on Crete and offshore islands.

children.name: Ovis
children.description: The wild sheep — seven species of stocky, mountain-dwelling bovids found from the Atlantic islands through Europe, Central Asia, and North America, prized by hunters for their massive, coiled horns and by humans for wool. The domestic sheep (O. aries) was derived from the mouflon around 10,000–11,000 years ago and today numbers over one billion — more than any other domestic ruminant.

children.name: Ovis aries
children.description: The mouflon is both the ancestor of the domestic sheep and a wild species still surviving in mountain refugia. The ancestral wild population is thought to have been from the Caucasus and southwestern Asia; feral island populations on Cyprus, Sardinia, and Corsica — introduced in prehistoric times — are in fact semi-feral descendants of early domestic sheep that have re-wilded over millennia. Males bear impressive coiled horns; the reddish-brown coat has a pale saddle patch in winter. In many European countries, mouflon were re-introduced as game animals and have established feral populations in the Alps, Pyrenees, and central European highlands. Domestic sheep in all their hundreds of breeds are classified as the same species.

children.name: Syncerus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the African buffalo, one of Africa's most formidable large herbivores, living in herds of up to a thousand animals across sub-Saharan savannas, forests, and floodplains.

children.name: Tragelaphus
children.description: The spiral-horned antelopes of sub-Saharan Africa — a genus of 8 large bovids of forests and dense bush, all displaying the characteristic twisted horns and white-striped or spotted flanks that define the Tragelaphini tribe.

children.name: Tragelaphus scriptus
children.description: The Bushbuck is the most widespread of the spiral-horned antelopes, found across sub-Saharan Africa from Senegal to Ethiopia and south to South Africa wherever dense undergrowth, forest edges, and riparian thickets occur. Solitary and largely nocturnal, it is one of the few antelopes that thrives near human settlements if cover is available. Males have twisted horns and striking white spots and stripes on a chestnut coat, while females are hornless and more uniformly reddish-brown.

children.name: Tragelaphus strepsiceros
children.description: The Greater Kudu is Africa's tallest antelope after the giraffe, with males carrying magnificent spiralling horns that can exceed 1.8 m in length — used in combat by interlocking and twisting. It inhabits dry woodland, thornbush, and rocky hillsides from East Africa to southern Africa, where it browses on leaves, flowers, and pods. Despite being heavily hunted for its exceptional trophy horns, it remains relatively common and adapts well to farmland if cover is present.

children.name: Tragelaphus imberbis
children.description: The Lesser Kudu is a slender, shy antelope of arid and semi-arid thornbush in East Africa, primarily in Ethiopia, Somalia, Kenya, and Tanzania, where it relies on dense acacia scrub for cover and browses on a wide range of leaves and fruits to meet its water requirements without drinking. Males display 10–14 lateral white stripes on their flanks and two prominent white throat patches. Near Threatened due to habitat degradation and hunting.

children.name: Tragelaphus eurycerus
children.description: The Bongo is the largest forest antelope in the world, instantly recognisable by its vivid chestnut-red coat marked with 10–15 bold white vertical stripes, a white muzzle, and — uniquely for the genus — horns borne by both sexes. It inhabits dense tropical rainforest from West Africa through the Congo Basin to Kenya's montane forests, where it browses on leaves, bark, and roots. The highly isolated eastern mountain subspecies (T. e. isaaci) in Kenya's Aberdare Range and Mount Kenya is Critically Endangered with fewer than 100 individuals remaining in the wild.

children.name: Tragelaphus angasii
children.description: The Nyala is among the most sexually dimorphic of all bovids: males are shaggy, slate-grey with white stripes and a yellow-orange lower body, standing almost 1.2 m at the shoulder with deeply spiralled horns, while females are bright chestnut with clear stripes and no horns. It inhabits dense lowland forest and riparian thickets in southeastern Africa from southern Malawi to KwaZulu-Natal. Named after George French Angas, an Australian naturalist who described it in 1849.

children.name: Tragelaphus spekii
children.description: The Sitatunga is the most aquatic of Africa's antelopes, inhabiting permanently flooded papyrus swamps, reed beds, and riverine forests across central and western Africa, from Senegal to Ethiopia and south to Zambia. Its elongated, splayed hooves, shaggy waterproof coat, and powerful hindquarters enable it to wade and swim through deep marsh vegetation in search of aquatic plants. Males bear gently twisted horns and a dark chocolate coat with faint white stripes; females are smaller and bright reddish-brown. Named after explorer John Hanning Speke.

children.name: Tragelaphus oryx
children.description: The Common Eland is the world's largest antelope, with old bulls reaching 700–950 kg — yet despite this bulk it is remarkably agile and can clear a 2 m fence from a standstill. It inhabits open woodland, savanna, and montane grasslands across eastern and southern Africa, grazing and browsing with equal facility and obtaining sufficient water from its food to survive in arid regions. Both sexes carry spirally twisted horns, and a diagnostic dewlap develops in males with age. It has been semi-domesticated for meat and milk production in several African countries.

children.name: Tragelaphus derbianus
children.description: The Giant Eland surpasses even the Common Eland in shoulder height, with males reaching 1.76 m, and is the largest of all spiral-horned antelopes by stature. It inhabits woodland savanna and gallery forest in the Sahel belt from Senegal and The Gambia east to Sudan and the Central African Republic. The massive V-shaped horns of males can reach 1.2 m in length. The western subspecies (T. d. derbianus) is Critically Endangered with fewer than 200 individuals. Named after Edward Smith-Stanley, 13th Earl of Derby, a renowned Victorian naturalist and collector.
